item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I headcanon this as the beginning of the Multiversal-Foundation alliance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, I read the last footnote thingy, and it showed a bunch more footnotes inside it, but I can't read them because when I take my mouse off the original note, the text disappears. What do they say?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good, because I was starting to think that [ACCESS DENIED] was a new title of this article, and I was starting to think it made sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was accidentally removed, but it's been reinstated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, wasn't the title "Technical Difficulties"? Why is it staying as "Access Denied"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who works in IT I found this article great, I've seen footnotes like this in real life but in code comments.

as for the footnotes making it hard to read the article, I had no problems as I can just hover over the notation to get a tool tip of it, but if your reading this on a phone and can't do that I see the point, it's hardly the worst offender that's stuck around tho.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Classification policies of the Foundation within SCP-2242-1 are significantly more cautious.

In 9 cases, Items classified as Keter by our Foundation have been designated Double-Keter by SCP-2242-1's Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will upvote this one. You made a joke of the wifi footnote's system, so LOL.
On the other hand, I imagined the foundation to be in panic rather than so cooperative with the -1.

_So, how are you there?
_ I'm not at liberty to discuss such matter.
_Me neither.
_…
_…
Redacted1 has disconnected from chat

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea and I think the piece is well written. I like that the LAN is set up to isolate this breach to one network but I think it could use some more containment. Possibly using a D-class to read it first to make sure the 'paranoid' Foundation on the other end hasnt slapped memetic kill agents onto Euclid class documents after a security scare.

People have said it before, but shit that can go wrong WILL go wrong in the SCP universe. Imagine a virus infects the alternate Foundations computers, imagine SCP-033 or SCP-633 got loose on their system and got across to FOS 14.1. If SCP-033 got into the system any eligible medium in that room could become corrupted as well because its not a contingency that this containment procedure is prepared for.

I'd advise you look at a couple of SCP's that could screw with yours and add some contingencies in to deal with them ^^ All in all I actually do like it but it could do with a little more :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. It's a little frustrating, because I've never seen that before, but page source was of course what I consulted first. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy the idea of a gimmick, but you don't do much with it here besides merely use it.

This is one of our many articles recently that expects to survive merely by showing up and being well-written. It doesn't do much that's interesting; it's nothing I haven't seen before plenty of times. It has a little bit of fun, but not really very much.

But, indeed, it's in the positives right now, and may well survive, and the comment above cites "well-written and on-point tonally" as part of the reason for being upvoted. That is… not great for quality standards, since being well-written with good tone should be the minimum standard for existing on the site, but what can ya do.

A nitpick unrelated to quality: It's kind of weird to note the existence of the alternate universe being treated as such a big deal.

Another nitpick unrelated to quality: I don't like the use of Rosen, aka the character whose concept is a meta-joke that he's utter shit at his job (and indeed that seems to be how he's used here), but I think that's mainly personal. It wouldn't merit a downvote from me, because it's hard enough to get people to stop freaking out about using named characters or avatars in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this one a lot. I didn't really grok the footnotes differences until pretty far into the article and had to go back for clarity's sake, but it wasn't a Herculean trial.

Edit: I'd also say this isn't a case of the format screw/bit of coding really making the article, but it was an interesting supporting element.

(Also, I wasn't sure if you were referencing David Rosen/IT stuff straight or distancing it in another universe, but it was amusing either way.)

The reason I like this, aside from it being well-written and on-point tonally, is that it falls into both the categories of "Weird shit just happens around the Foundation." and "The Foundation has to document everything." It's not quite to the level of absurd daily hijinks in, say, the S & C Plastics canon, but it's still nice to see a bit of slice-of-life going around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I meant by that was that, with this being the SCPverse, if something bad could happen, it probably will. So they got lucky once, in that the first universe they ended up being connected to is relatively benign. Any further connections, which the addendum tells us is already starting to happen, should probably end poorly.

The Foundation is one of the the most powerful, if not THE most powerful, organizations on the planet. They have access to capabilities that no one else could dream of. If the Foundation wanted to move between realities, I'm almost certain they could. If the timelines are very similar, the same must be true for the other Foundation. An ally can easily become an enemy, and they have access to all your computer systems. Maybe I am overreacting, but surely that is something that would be taken into account here. The Foundation have back up plans for their back up plans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is alright, but I hate how it has to be footnotes. It makes it confusing, hard to read, and absolutely destroys the flow of the article. The idea of two universes contacting one another could have been done a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The article is really well written and in-tone of the foundation. The execution is genius but the idea is not great as well.
No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like to see less "our" and "us"; refer to baseline Foundation as baseline or something, which I'm sure the other Foundation would object to, but still.

I struggled with this during drafting, and at one point even had the baseline designated as 2242-A to deal with it. You're right, it still needs work.

And second, that sixth reference's extra footnotes don't seem accessible in any way. Not sure if you meant that.

It's intentional. Those are actually not real footnotes anyway, as Wikidot only allows for one set of footnotes and one set of bibliographic references. Page Source reveals all, at the low low cost of breaking immersion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Odds should be that whoever else is getting a look in at their computer systems has some malevolent intent.

Given that this only currently connects them to one other Foundation from a very similar reality, why should the odds be that this other-Foundation would have malevolent intent? There's also the limitations that would prevent other-Foundation from doing anything, beyond maybe inserting cognitohazardous images/content into the notations.

There was no intent to trivialize the phenomenon, so I'll review and consider whether that's incidentally come across. The final addendum could probably use some elaboration, so thanks for the comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Largely agreeing with this criticism. It's not done with any particularly innovative things that grab my attention, nor is the writing something that overcomes that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. I am aware of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The references are this alternate universe communicating with this universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Regardless of the similarities, there are still plenty of disastrous things that could go wrong with another similarly powerful organisation knowing all your secrets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I'm a sucker for alternate realities (especially in the Foundationverse) I can't say that anything particularly interesting occurs in this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The other network is an alternate reality Foundation with the same goals and interest in breaking the link, which makes a huge difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Disclaimer: Long time reader but new to the community, so maybe take what I say with a pinch of salt)

I really like the core idea of a mundane software bug causing an anomalous effect, but I found myself not really enjoying this one very much. The execution trivialises the anomaly and there seems to be an undertone of flippancy about the whole thing. Having your computer system, which stores some of the most secret information on the planet, linked with another strikes me as a huge issue. The final addendum really bothered me as well. The problem is getting worse, potentially allowing multiple other universes/timelines access to all stored data, and the response is essentially "meh, it's probably fine". In the SCPverse, they beat the odds that the first place they were linked to didn't immediately start trying to murder (or worse) everyone. Odds should be that whoever else is getting a look in at their computer systems has some malevolent intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reading the alternate references as the work of a very frustrated IT manager now having to deal with two networks full of people trying to break his computers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent meta-article, and I love how the two universes differ in more or less wholly unimportant ways. No "Hitler won" or "world war three" nonsense; you don't need it to make another universe!

Two thoughts: I'd like to see less "our" and "us"; refer to baseline Foundation as baseline or something, which I'm sure the other Foundation would object to, but still. And second, that sixth reference's extra footnotes don't seem accessible in any way. Not sure if you meant that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I already expressed my thoughts on this, but for clarity:

I dislike this largely due to the gimmick that you're using to convey the story of these two Foundations communicating with each other. The references are aggravating to read, and it makes it hard to keep reading. The tone and character of the other Foundation seems very snarky (in a bad way) and is hard to want to care for. There's also the question of this playing out in a document, when… why not use a test page for that? It makes the whole thing seem unprofessional.

The anomaly itself, while interesting, doesn't feel like it plays out this well with all of the above. There's story potential here, but the article is frustrating to work with and read to get any sense of involvement in it at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
fuck you and your footnotes
This is really good, and I enjoyed it. +1
seriously though footnotes ;-;

item.title: Footnotes all the way down.
item.content:encoded: New skip! Enjoy, folks. Special thanks to Live2Die, MrWrong, SoundChaser, TwistedGears, Macleod, Dr_Leonard, Decibelle, and anyone else I missed who looked at this in chat or on the forums. Additionally, shoutouts to Wikidot1 for letting me mess with stuff in so many silly ways.
